title: AWS Details How One Customer Scaled to One Million Lambda Functions
gist: AWS details how ProGlove, an industrial-wearables maker, scaled its SaaS to over 1 million Lambda functions across thousands of one-account-per-tenant AWS accounts, keeping idle cost under $1/month per account via CloudFormation StackSets automation, jittered scheduling, and aggressive scale-to-zero. The case shows cost and quota visibility driving architecture before capacity crunches hit.

insights.claim: ProGlove isolated each customer in a dedicated AWS account, trading operational overhead for stronger security boundaries, independent service quotas, and clear per-customer cost attribution.
insights.speaker: Matt Foster
insights.so_what: A reference architecture for multi-tenant SaaS isolation, with an explicit tradeoff (overhead vs. hard boundaries) relevant to SaaS platform design decisions.
insights.quote: trading additional operational overhead for stronger security boundaries, independent service quotas, and clear per-customer cost attribution
insights.locator: ProGlove chose to isolate each customer

insights.claim: The per-tenant model introduced significant operational friction once the platform surpassed 50 accounts, forcing a shift to automated provisioning.
insights.speaker: Matt Foster
insights.so_what: Marks ~50 accounts as the threshold where manual per-tenant operations break down—a planning signal for SaaS scaling.
insights.quote: the decision introduced operational friction once the platform surpassed 50 accounts

insights.claim: ProGlove combined AWS Organizations, Step Functions, and CloudFormation StackSets into a single pipeline that creates and updates every account, later collaborating with AWS engineers to raise StackSet throughput to fan changes across thousands of accounts hosting 1M+ Lambda functions.
insights.speaker: Matt Foster
insights.so_what: Demonstrates the AWS-native toolchain for fleet-scale multi-account management—and that StackSet throughput can be a bottleneck requiring AWS engineering help.
insights.quote: increased StackSet throughput so the mechanism could fan changes across thousands of accounts

insights.claim: StackSet rollouts remained slower than single-account CloudFormation deployments and centralizing logs reduced per-tenant granularity, tradeoffs the team accepted for isolation and cost visibility.
insights.speaker: Matt Foster
insights.so_what: Honest cost of fleet automation—slower rollouts, less granular observability—useful for teams weighing centralized vs. per-tenant tooling.
insights.quote: StackSet rollouts remained slower than single-account CloudFormation deployments
insights.locator: The AWS post also notes that automation

insights.claim: Early cron-style schedules triggered the same function in every account at identical times, producing a self-inflicted DDoS that was fixed by replacing rigid timers with jittered execution windows and event-driven triggers.
insights.speaker: Matt Foster
insights.so_what: A cautionary pattern: synchronized cron across a large fleet self-DDoSes; jitter and event-driven triggers are the fix—applicable to any large serverless deployment.
insights.quote: producing what the engineers describe as a self-inflicted DDoS

insights.claim: Per-function logs and metrics multiplied across thousands of accounts threatened to dominate the bill, so ProGlove consolidated high-priority failures into a central dead-letter queue and removed unused SQS queues, cutting per-account idle spend to under one US dollar.
insights.speaker: Matt Foster
insights.so_what: Observability spend can dominate serverless bills at fleet scale; the sub-$1/month idle cost is a concrete efficiency benchmark.
insights.quote: these changes reduced per-account idle spend to under one US dollar
insights.locator: Observability costs followed a similar trajectory

insights.claim: Across ProGlove, Capital One, and DoorDash, the common thread is that cost and quota visibility drive architectural choices long before capacity crunches appear.
insights.speaker: Matt Foster
insights.so_what: Generalizes a cross-company pattern—treat cost/quota visibility as the leading design constraint for serverless at scale.
insights.quote: the common thread is that cost and quota visibility drive architectural choices long before capacity crunches appear

insights.claim: DoorDash's serverless migration kept more than ten million daily API calls from overrunning concurrency limits through disciplined automation.
insights.speaker: Matt Foster
insights.so_what: A comparative scale data point reinforcing automation as the lever for serverless concurrency management.
insights.quote: highlighting that disciplined automation kept more than ten million daily API calls from overrunning concurrency limits
